title: Recognizing Deterioration: Rapid Response and the Chain of Command
subtitle: A 67-year-old on the floor with pneumonia develops a rising respiratory rate, falling oxygen, hypotension, and new confusion. The nurse must recognize the deterioration, activate the rapid response team, and escalate to rescue the patient before arrest.

patient.name: Gerald Stack
patient.admitDate: Medical unit, admitted with pneumonia. Over the last hour: respiratory rate climbing, SpO2 dropping, blood pressure falling, and new acute confusion with low urine output — meeting rapid-response criteria. Nurse recognizing deterioration and activating the team.

vitals.temp: O2 applied; IV access; RRT at bedside
Stabilizing; transfer to higher level of care
Progressed to arrest — failure to rescue

labs.v: RR, SpO2, BP, mental status, UOP.

nursesNote: Gerald Stack, 67, admitted with pneumonia, now deteriorating: RR 30+, SpO2 88%, BP 86/54, HR 118, new confusion, and low urine output — meeting rapid-response criteria. Subtle changes (especially a rising respiratory rate) often precede arrest by hours; early recognition enables rescue. Activate the rapid response team WITHOUT delay (don't wait to 'see if it improves'); stay with the patient and support ABCs (oxygen, positioning, monitor, IV access). Communicate with SBAR and coordinate roles with closed-loop communication. If a provider is unresponsive or the plan is inadequate, escalate up the chain of command to prevent failure to rescue. Document and reassess.
providerOrders: Recognize deterioration via early warning signs; activate the rapid response team
Stay with the patient; support airway, breathing, circulation; apply oxygen and monitor
Establish IV access; obtain vitals/labs; bring the chart and medication list
Communicate with SBAR; assign roles with closed-loop communication
Escalate up the chain of command if the response is delayed or inadequate

opts.text: Subtle changes often precede arrest by hours; early recognition enables rescue
Respiratory rate is one of the most sensitive early signs of deterioration
New confusion, falling SpO2, hypotension, and low urine output are warning signs
A Rapid Response Team brings critical-care help to the bedside before arrest
It is best to wait and see if the patient improves before calling for help
Any staff member (and often family) can activate a rapid response
Activating the RRT early helps prevent "failure to rescue"
SBAR provides a clear, structured handoff to the responding team
The nurse stays with the patient, supports ABCs, and gathers information
If a provider is unresponsive or the plan is unsafe, escalate up the chain of command
Calling a rapid response is a sign of failure and should be avoided
Closed-loop communication and role assignment keep the response organized
Recognize the deterioration and quickly assess ABCs
Activate the rapid response team and call for help
Stay with the patient: oxygen, positioning, monitor, IV access
Give an SBAR report and coordinate roles as the team arrives
Escalate via chain of command if needed; document and reassess

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Does the finding warrant a rapid response?

opts.text: New respiratory rate of 30+ with falling SpO2
New acute confusion / altered mental status
Systolic BP dropping into the 80s with tachycardia
Urine output under 30 mL/hr for several hours
A nurse's strong sense that the patient is "just not right"
Stable vitals at baseline, alert and comfortable
Mild incisional pain relieved by analgesia with normal vitals
Expected mild fatigue with normal vital signs

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the patient and confirm the deteriorating signs
Activate the rapid response team without delay
Provide immediate interventions (oxygen, positioning, monitoring)
Hand off with SBAR and assign clear roles
Escalate up the chain if the response is inadequate; document

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.sub: Supports timely rescue or delays it?

opts.text: The nurse activates the RRT at the first signs of deterioration
SBAR clearly communicates the situation to the team
The nurse stays with the patient, gives oxygen, and assigns roles
When the provider doesn't respond, the nurse escalates up the chain
The nurse waits an hour "to see if it improves" before calling
The deterioration is documented but no one is notified
An inadequate phone order is accepted without escalating worsening signs
Early warning signs are dismissed as "probably nothing"
